exemplar.title: To what extent do Euphoria and Mean Girls compare and contrast in costume design to convey the feminist film theory?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do Euphoria and Mean Girls compare and contrast in costume design to convey the feminist film theory?

strengths: The topic is articulated clearly, with a defined focus on costume design and feminist film theory in two texts
The research question is stated prominently and threaded through the introduction and conclusion
A generally appropriate range of sources and methods (film screenshots, designer interviews, academic texts) shows informed selection
weaknesses: The research question remains somewhat broad and occasionally yields descriptive digressions
The investigation lacks a fully systematic methodological explanation (no explicit analytical framework or selection criteria)
At times the essay wanders into general commentary on hyper-sexualisation rather than consistently tying observations back to the RQ
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall solidly into the middle of the 3â4 band: topic and purpose are communicated effectively but risk diffuseness; the RQ is clear yet only partially focused; source selection is relevant but methodologically under-specified. A score of 4/6 reflects this competent yet not fully rigorous performance.

strengths: Demonstrates a sound grasp of core feminist film theory concepts (Mulveyâs male gaze, self-objectification)
Uses film terminology (mise-en-scÃ¨ne, hyper-sexualisation) correctly in most instances
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on non-academic sources (fandom wikis, popular journalism) limits scholarly depth
Occasional imprecision in terminology (e.g., conflating objectivity with objectification) and underdeveloped conceptual nuances
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge of theory and terminology is clear but unevenly supported by academic literature, placing performance in the lower half of the 3â4 band. A 3/6 acknowledges solid subject knowledge alongside inconsistent scholarly rigor.

strengths: Research is broadly relevant, drawing on visual, interview, and theoretical sources that address the RQ
Analysis links costume elements (colour, silhouette) to feminist concepts and power dynamics with insight
Visual comparative analyses (e.g., Fig. 2, Fig. 9) are compelling and reinforce the theoretical frame
weaknesses: Some analyses remain surface-level and descriptive, lacking sustained interrogation of production context
Evaluative criteria for judging the âextentâ of feminist expression are not fully articulated
Reliance on tertiary sources without critical interrogation of their credibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three CT strands demonstrate good, mostly supported analysis and discussion that advances the argument, but fall short of the depth and critical sophistication of the upper band. Awarding 6/12 reflects solid critical thinking at the lower-middle of the 4â6 band.

strengths: Structure meets EE requirements with a title page, table of contents, clear headings, and logical sectioning
Consistent formatting of images, captions, and a works-cited list supports readability
weaknesses: Occasional inconsistent citation style and minor typographical errors detract from polish
Some images are oversized and interrupt the text flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation adheres to IB conventions overall, with only minor lapses in polish. A score of 3/4 is appropriate for a well-structured but not perfectly formatted submission.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student provides a formatted title page, consistent heading styles, in-text citations and an extensive works-cited list. Images are embedded with figures and captions, and a table of contents with page numbers is present. The word count is stated and within limits.

Minor issuesâsome oversized images, occasional typographical errors, and a lack of uniform citation style in placesâdetract from complete polish, but most layout and formatting conventions are correctly applied, justifying placement in the 3â4 band with a solid score of 3.

The essay assembles a fair quantity of materialâstill images from both texts, designer interviews, academic theory, and comparative references to other teen films. Most sources relate to costume design or feminist theory, giving the research reasonable relevance to the RQ.

Nonetheless, reliance on tertiary sources (fandom wikis, popular media sites) and limited critical interrogation of their credibility keep the work from the âgood-to-excellentâ range. The breadth is adequate and mostly pertinent, matching the lower end of the 4â6 band.

The student employs a reasonably wide range of material: primary screenshots from both films/series, interviews with costume designers, academic texts by Mulvey and Ruti, and some popular articles. Source selection is therefore âgenerally relevantâ, and the Works Cited list demonstrates an attempt at breadth.

Nevertheless, methodological explanation is limited. The essay does not articulate how sources were selected or how images were analysed beyond descriptive commentary, nor does it outline any systematic approach (e.g., scene selection criteria, analytical framework). This indicates only partial evidence of informed and effective selection, meriting the 3â4 band but not its uppermost level.

The discussion culminates in a clear summative statement recognising the âmoderate extentâ to which the two works express feminist film theory, and it notes exceptions (Rue, Janis) as counterpoints. This shows some attempt at evaluation and balance.

Yet the evaluative component is uneven: criteria for judging âextentâ are not fully articulated, and reflections on limitations, alternative readings, or production contexts are brief. The argument is coherent but occasionally repetitive and more confirmatory than critical. These features align with the descriptors for adequate-to-good discussion and evaluationâhence the lower end of the 4â6 band.

The essay follows a conventional EE structure: title page with word count, abstract-style introduction, logically ordered sections, conclusion, and works cited. Headings and a table of contents assist navigation, and images are captioned.

Some structural weaknesses are evidentâlengthy plot summary sections interrupt analytical flow, section balance is uneven, and methodological explanation is under-developedâbut overall the structure complies with EE requirements and supports reader comprehension, meriting a mark in the top presentation band.

The student demonstrates a sound grasp of key ideas in feminist film theory, referring accurately to Mulveyâs male gaze, self-objectification, and second-wave feminism. The discussion of specific costume choices shows awareness of how mise-en-scÃ¨ne contributes to meaning.

Yet much supporting evidence comes from non-academic sources (fandom wikis, popular journalism). The engagement with scholarly literature is sporadic and often treated descriptively rather than critically. Consequently, the knowledge displayed is clear but not consistently sophisticated, justifying placement in the 3â4 band at the midpoint.

The student moves beyond mere description by linking specific costume elements (colour palettes, silhouettes, exposure of skin) to notions of the male gaze and character power dynamics, and by drawing contrasts between male and female costuming. These observations show insight and some originality.

However, many claims remain surface-level, with limited exploration of wider cinematic context, narrative function or production constraints, and some assertions lack robust evidence. The analysis supports conclusions âmostlyâ, but not with the sustained depth or nuanced argumentation expected for higher bands. Thus, a score in the 4â6 range, towards its lower end, is appropriate.

The research question is presented verbatim on p. 1 and revisited in the introduction and conclusion, establishing a visible thread through the essay. However, the formulation combines two productions, multiple characters and an expansive theory, resulting in only partial focus. Several sections (e.g., long plot summaries) do not tie back sharply to the question, suggesting that the connection between RQ and subsequent discussion is uneven.

Overall, the clarity of wording is sound, but the degree of focus and sustained alignment with the debate is moderate rather than rigorous, aligning with the middle of the 3â4 band.

Film-specific terms such as âmise-en-scÃ¨neâ, âmale gazeâ, âhyper-sexualisationâ, âcostume designâ and âfeminist film theoryâ are used correctly in most cases, indicating a generally secure command of the discourse. The student also attempts to integrate theoretical vocabulary from Mulvey and Ruti.

However, usage is occasionally repetitive (âfeminist film theoryâ is invoked as a catch-all) and sometimes imprecise (e.g., conflating âobjectivityâ with âobjectificationâ). Conceptual nuances are not always fully developed, so the work fits the âmostly accurateâ descriptor of the 3â4 band rather than the consistent precision required for 5â6.

annotations.comment: The Halloween costume comparison (Fig. 15) is creative, but the essay lacks reflection on how costume selection was influenced by external cultural trends; adding such reflection would mark higher engagement.
The student correctly provides both the investigation title and a word count on the title page, satisfying key EE formatting requirements.
The promotional image of Gretchen (Fig. 8) is well used to illustrate hyper-sexualized preppy aesthetic; this application shows consistent subject knowledge of costume semiotics.
The student could extend the application of JasiÅska et al.âs biological argument by critically discussing its ethical or cultural limitations within feminist film theory.
Fig. 9 compellingly contrasts Nateâs conservative attire with Cassieâs revealing costume, vividly exemplifying the power dynamic central to the feminist film theory critique.
Citation style in the works cited fluctuates between MLA, Chicago, and web formats; adopting a single, consistent style manual would improve presentation quality.

The juxtaposition of Maddyâs childlike lingerie (Fig. 4) and Lady Birdâs realistic bra (Fig. 6) demonstrates excellent critical evaluation of how costume design can support or resist objectification.

The section on Feminist Film Theory lacks a clear methodological statement explaining how theoretical concepts will be operationalized in the analysis; articulating an analytical framework would improve methodological rigor.

Repetition of the research question here reinforces coherence, but the follow-up summary remains descriptive rather than critically positioned; drawing explicit connections between each assertion and the RQ would deepen focus.
The side-by-side critique in Fig. 2 contrasts female hypersexualization with male modestyâa compelling comparative analysis that reinforces the feminist frame through visual evidence.
In Fig. 4, the timestamp citation is correct but inconsistent with other in-text citations; adopt a uniform citation style (e.g., MLA or Chicago) to ensure presentation consistency.

The background information consists largely of plot summary rather than analytical focus; reducing narrative detail and linking each character description to costume-theory dynamics would maintain relevance to the research question.

This usage of Naomi Tarrantâs quote is descriptive without evaluating its relevance or credibility; the student should critically engage with the source to demonstrate informed application of scholarly literature.

The analysis of Fig. 1 effectively links costume elements (colour, silhouette, exposure) to Nateâs male gaze, demonstrating clear understanding of how mise-en-scÃ¨ne constructs feminist theory arguments.

While the discussion links Bivensâ interview to design choices, it remains largely descriptive; embedding a critique of the designerâs intent and addressing broader production contexts would yield deeper analytical insight.
The inclusion of a detailed table of contents with clear section headings and page numbers supports reader navigation and demonstrates strong structural organization.

The research question as phrased âto what extent do â¦ compare and contrastâ is overly broad, combining multiple characters and theoretical strands; a more focused wording would sharpen scope and connection to subsequent analysis.

The Logic Behind Inquiry section outlines sample selection but lacks justification on criteria or sampling method; specifying how and why particular scenes and characters were chosen would evidence informed methodological selection.

The introduction opens with general film history rather than framing the investigationâs focus on feminist film theory and costume design; a sharper lead that directly situates the research question would strengthen focus and purpose.
Integrating the 1962 Sears catalogue (Fig. 5) is an innovative intertextual method that connects historical gendered clothing norms to modern costume designâevidencing creative critical thinking.

Fig. 7âs analysis of Lexiâs conservative wardrobe is insightful but would benefit from linking to specific feminist theory concepts (e.g., agency or resistance) to deepen crit
ical engagement.

The behind-the-scenes image (Fig. 12) suggests authentic production insight but lacks commentary on how costume design processes influenced analytical decisions; reflecting on decision-making would enhance engagement.

The conclusion summarizes findings but omits discussion of methodological limitations or alternative interpretations; addressing these would strengthen the evaluative component and critical reflexivity.
The disclaimer on mature themes is courteous, but such content notices may be unnecessary in the main text; consider incorporating content warnings in an appendix or footnote instead.
This scene-based analysis would gain depth by outlining selection criteria for these examples (e.g., frequency, narrative importance) to demonstrate systematic methodology.

Fig. 13âs collage effectively illustrates Maddyâs costume evolution across seasons and demonstrates strong analytical depth in linking wardrobe changes to character agency and self-objectification.

Fig. 18âs exploration of Julesâ hyper-feminine wardrobe and related quote provides a nuanced extension of feminist film theory to transgender representation, showcasing advanced critical thinking.

Fig. 3âs comparison to High School Musical is contextually interesting, but the rationale for selecting this particular text is not articulated; explaining its relevance to the RQ would clarify its methodological purpose.
Reliance on a commercial vintage catalog may weaken academic credibility; supplement with peer-reviewed studies on childlike costume semiotics to bolster evidence quality.
The composite of Rueâs outfits (Fig. 16) demonstrates sophisticated application of feminist theory to identify a consistent counter-example to objectification across her narrative arc.
